---
title: "Warm-up: reading and combining matrices"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

- Read a matrix: count its rows and columns, and pick out a single entry.
- Recognise special matrices: the zero matrix, the identity, and diagonal matrices.
- Combine matrices by **addition** (same shape) and by **scalar multiplication**.

No biology here. Just numbers. Take it slowly with pen and paper.

## The rule

A **matrix** is a rectangular grid of numbers. We write matrices with a capital
letter, like $A$, and we put the numbers inside square brackets:
$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 5 \\ 1 & 7 \end{bmatrix}.
$$

The **size** (or *dimensions*) of a matrix is written as **rows × columns**.
The matrix $A$ above has $2$ rows and $2$ columns, so it is a $2 \times 2$ matrix.
Always say the number of rows **first**.

A single number inside the matrix is called an **entry**. We name an entry by
its position: $a_{ij}$ means the entry in **row $i$, column $j$** (row first,
column second). For the matrix $A$ above:

- $a_{11} = 2$ (row 1, column 1),
- $a_{12} = 5$ (row 1, column 2),
- $a_{21} = 1$ (row 2, column 1),
- $a_{22} = 7$ (row 2, column 2).

A matrix with a single row is a **row vector**, and a matrix with a single
column is a **column vector**. We write vectors with a bold lowercase letter,
like $\mathbf{x}$:
$$
\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 8 \end{bmatrix} \quad (\text{a column vector, } 2 \times 1),
\qquad
\mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 6 & 9 \end{bmatrix} \quad (\text{a row vector, } 1 \times 3).
$$

Some special matrices to **recognise**:

- The **zero matrix** has every entry equal to $0$.
- A **diagonal matrix** has zeros everywhere except possibly on the diagonal
  (the entries $a_{11}, a_{22}, a_{33}, \dots$ running top-left to bottom-right).
- The **identity matrix** $I$ is a diagonal matrix whose diagonal entries are
  all $1$:
$$
I = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}.
$$

**Addition.** You can only add two matrices if they have the **same size**.
Just add the numbers in matching positions:
$$
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix}
+
\begin{bmatrix} 5 & 0 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 1+5 & 2+0 \\ 3+1 & 4+2 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 6 & 2 \\ 4 & 6 \end{bmatrix}.
$$

**Scalar multiplication.** A plain number multiplying a matrix is called a
**scalar**. Multiply **every** entry by that number:
$$
3 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 4 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 3\cdot 1 & 3\cdot 2 \\ 3\cdot 4 & 3\cdot 0 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 6 \\ 12 & 0 \end{bmatrix}.
$$

## Drills

Reading dimensions and entries.

**1.** What are the dimensions (rows × columns) of
$\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$?

**2.** What are the dimensions of
$\begin{bmatrix} 7 & 3 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$?

**3.** What are the dimensions of
$\begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 9 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$?

**4.** What are the dimensions of
$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$?

**5.** Is $\begin{bmatrix} 8 & 8 & 8 \end{bmatrix}$ a row vector or a column vector?

**6.** Is $\begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 6 \end{bmatrix}$ a row vector or a column vector?

For drills 7–9 use
$A = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 6 \\ 9 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$.

**7.** What is the entry $a_{11}$?

**8.** What is the entry $a_{21}$?

**9.** What is the entry $a_{12}$?

For drill 10 use
$B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 4 & 7 \\ 2 & 5 & 8 \\ 3 & 6 & 9 \end{bmatrix}$.

**10.** What is the entry $b_{23}$ (row 2, column 3)?

Recognising special matrices.

**11.** Is $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ the zero matrix, the
identity matrix, or neither?

**12.** Is $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ the
zero matrix, the identity matrix, or neither?

**13.** Is $\begin{bmatrix} 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$ a diagonal matrix?

Addition.

**14.** Compute
$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}
+
\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 5 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.

**15.** Compute
$\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 7 \\ 0 & 6 \end{bmatrix}
+
\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 5 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$.

**16.** Compute
$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}
+
\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$.

Scalar multiplication.

**17.** Compute
$2 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$.

**18.** Compute
$5 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$, then add the result to
$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.

## Check in R

You can build a matrix with [`matrix`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/matrix.html). By
default R fills **down the columns**, so use `byrow = TRUE` to type it the way it
looks on paper. Use [`dim`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/dim.html) for the dimensions
and `A[i, j]` to read an entry.

```r
A <- matrix(c(3, 6,
              9, 2), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
dim(A)      # 2 2   (drill 7-9 matrix: 2 rows, 2 cols)
A[1, 1]     # 3     (this is a_11)
A[2, 1]     # 9     (this is a_21)

# Addition (drill 14)
matrix(c(1, 0, 2, 3), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE) +
matrix(c(4, 5, 1, 1), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
#      [,1] [,2]
# [1,]    5    5
# [2,]    3    4

# Scalar multiplication (drill 17)
2 * matrix(c(1, 3, 0, 4), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
#      [,1] [,2]
# [1,]    2    6
# [2,]    0    8
```

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 1 — rows first, then columns"}
Dimensions are always written **rows × columns**. Count the horizontal layers
(rows) first, then how many numbers sit in one row (columns).
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 2 — reading a_ij"}
In $a_{ij}$ the **first** number is the row and the **second** is the column.
So $a_{21}$ means "go to row 2, then column 1". Point with two fingers if it helps.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 3 — special matrices"}
Zero matrix: every entry is $0$. Identity: $1$ on the diagonal, $0$ everywhere
else. Diagonal: $0$ everywhere off the diagonal, but the diagonal numbers can be
anything.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 4 — addition needs matching shapes"}
Only add matrices of the **same** size. Then add position-by-position: top-left
to top-left, and so on.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 5 — scalar hits everything"}
A scalar multiplies **every single entry**, with no exceptions.
:::

## Step-by-step solutions

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solutions"}

**1.** The matrix has 2 rows and 2 columns, so it is $2 \times 2$.

**2.** One row, three numbers in it, so $1 \times 3$.

**3.** Three numbers stacked in a single column, so $3 \times 1$.

**4.** Two rows, three columns, so $2 \times 3$.

**5.** It has one row, so it is a **row vector**.

**6.** It has one column, so it is a **column vector**.

**7.** $a_{11}$ is row 1, column 1, which is $3$.

**8.** $a_{21}$ is row 2, column 1, which is $9$.

**9.** $a_{12}$ is row 1, column 2, which is $6$.

**10.** $b_{23}$ is row 2, column 3. Row 2 is $\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 5 & 8 \end{bmatrix}$,
and its third number is $8$. So $b_{23} = 8$.

**11.** Every entry is $0$, so it is the **zero matrix**.

**12.** It has $1$ on the diagonal and $0$ elsewhere, so it is the **identity matrix**.

**13.** The only nonzero entries ($5$ and $2$) sit on the diagonal, and the
off-diagonal entries are $0$. Yes, it is a **diagonal matrix**.

**14.** Add matching positions:
$$
\begin{bmatrix} 1+4 & 0+5 \\ 2+1 & 3+1 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 5 & 5 \\ 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix}.
$$

**15.** Add matching positions:
$$
\begin{bmatrix} 2+3 & 7+1 \\ 0+5 & 6+2 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 5 & 8 \\ 5 & 8 \end{bmatrix}.
$$

**16.** Both are $1 \times 3$, so add entry by entry:
$$
\begin{bmatrix} 1+4 & 2+0 & 3+6 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 5 & 2 & 9 \end{bmatrix}.
$$

**17.** Multiply every entry by $2$:
$$
\begin{bmatrix} 2\cdot 1 & 2\cdot 3 \\ 2\cdot 0 & 2\cdot 4 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 6 \\ 0 & 8 \end{bmatrix}.
$$

**18.** First the scalar multiplication:
$$
5 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 10 & 0 \\ 5 & 15 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
Then add $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$:
$$
\begin{bmatrix} 10+1 & 0+1 \\ 5+1 & 15+1 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 11 & 1 \\ 6 & 16 \end{bmatrix}.
$$

:::
